title: Athena's New Data
tagline: She's been tracking Achilles's heel arc for two seasons. The trend is not good. She has to tell him.
hook_line: Athena has three scrolls of data on Achilles's ankle. She's been tracking it since he first mentioned it. The trend is clear. The ankle is getting worse, not better. And she has to tell him that today.

scenes.title: The Three Scrolls
scenes.narration: Athena spent the night before reading all three scrolls from beginning to end. She'd never read them in sequence — only added to them, episode by episode. Reading them in order was different. The pattern was clear. The ankle had been accommodated. The training had shifted. The role had evolved. And through all of it, steadily, across every measurement she'd taken, the ankle was worse. Not dramatically. Not suddenly. Just consistently, persistently, measurably worse. She rolled the scrolls and sat with them for a while before breakfast.
scenes.visual_prompt: Athena at her desk late at night, all three scrolls unrolled and laid end to end — the full record visible as a continuous document, the earliest entries showing small measurements, the later entries showing the trend — a line graph of ankle condition over time visible in the data she's collected, declining steadily — Athena reading the end of the third scroll and then looking back at the beginning of the first — Archie on the desk beside her, awake at this hour, watching her — the lamplight warm and quiet, the weight of the data in the silence — #3D2B00 and warm lamp amber, the scrolls detailed, the night quiet

scenes.title: Finding Achilles
scenes.narration: Athena found Achilles at the coordination post after morning training. She had the three scrolls with her. She said: 'Can we talk?' Achilles looked at the scrolls. He knew which scrolls those were. He'd known she was keeping them — he'd noticed, early on, that she wrote notes after she watched him train. He hadn't said anything. 'Yes,' he said. He sat on the training post bench. Athena sat beside him. She didn't unroll the scrolls immediately. 'How does it feel?' she asked. 'Worse,' he said. 'I know.'
scenes.visual_prompt: Athena approaching Achilles at the coordination post, the three scrolls visible under her arm — Achilles seeing the scrolls and knowing exactly what they are — the two of them sitting side by side on the training post bench, not facing each other, both looking forward — Athena's question 'how does it feel?' / Achilles's answer 'worse' / Athena's 'I know' shown through the specific quality of the exchange: two people who already know what the conversation is about — the scrolls between them on the bench, not yet opened — morning light on the training ground, #FFD700 and honest morning quality, the weight in the ordinariness of the setting

scenes.title: What the Data Shows
scenes.narration: Athena showed him. Not all three scrolls at once — just the most recent one, with the measurements. The trend line. The rate of change. Achilles looked at it for a long time. 'It's slow,' he said. 'Yes,' Athena said. 'But it's consistent.' 'Yes.' 'And it's not going to stop.' Athena didn't answer immediately. 'I don't know that,' she said carefully. 'I know what I've measured.' He nodded. 'Thank you for telling me what you measured.'
scenes.visual_prompt: Athena and Achilles with the third scroll open between them — the trend data visible, the line declining consistently — Achilles studying it with the focused attention he gives everything — the exchange: 'it's slow' / 'yes' / 'but consistent' / 'yes' / 'it's not going to stop' / Athena's careful 'I don't know that. I know what I've measured' — the distinction between 'what the data shows' and 'what will happen' shown in Athena's precision — Achilles's acknowledgment: 'thank you for telling me what you measured' — no drama, no collapse, two serious people looking at real information — morning light, #FFD700, the scroll detail readable

scenes.title: Artemis Already Knew
scenes.narration: Artemis arrived at the training ground for her morning practice. She looked at Achilles sitting with Athena and the scrolls and said: 'You're showing him the data.' 'Yes,' Athena said. Artemis sat on the other side of Achilles. 'I've been tracking it too,' she said. She had a small notebook — not scrolls, a folded leather notebook she carried during training. She put it on the bench. It was half full. Achilles looked at the notebook. He looked at the scrolls. He looked at Artemis. 'How long?' 'Since the second week,' Artemis said. 'You weren't ready to know then.'
scenes.visual_prompt: Artemis arriving and joining them — the three of them on the bench: Athena with the scrolls, Achilles in the middle, Artemis with her small leather training notebook — the notebook placed beside the scrolls — the two parallel records: Athena's formal data and Artemis's field observations — Achilles looking from one to the other — 'how long?' / 'since the second week. You weren't ready to know then.' — Achilles processing being watched by two people, both of whom were trying to protect him in their own way — Archie appearing on the fence post above them, having been watching the whole time — warm morning, #FFD700 and the specific intimacy of this bench

scenes.title: What He Asks
scenes.narration: Achilles was quiet for a while. Then he asked the question that had been waiting: 'How much time do I have where it's still useful?' Neither Athena nor Artemis answered immediately. 'That depends on how you use it,' Athena said finally. 'And what you call useful,' Artemis said. Achilles nodded slowly. 'I've been building the coordination role,' he said. 'I know,' Athena said. 'It's working,' Artemis said. He nodded again. 'Then that's what I should keep building.' There was a pause. 'Yes,' Athena said. 'Yes,' Artemis said. They stayed on the bench for a while.
scenes.visual_prompt: The three of them on the bench in the morning light — the question 'how much time do I have where it's still useful?' in the air — Athena and Artemis's answers: 'that depends on how you use it' / 'and what you call useful' — Achilles's conclusion: 'then that's what I should keep building' — the dual 'yes' from both of them — the bench as the space for this, not dramatic, just three serious people who care about each other being honest in the morning sun — Archie on the fence above still watching, his presence a quiet third witness to the whole conversation — the scrolls and notebook on the bench between them, closed now — #FFD700 and morning warmth, the resolution in the staying-on-the-bench

scenes.title: Zeus Asks
scenes.narration: Zeus found Achilles at lunch. He'd seen the three of them on the bench. He didn't ask what they'd talked about. He said: 'The coordination drills are getting better.' 'Yes,' Achilles said. 'The reaction time is down to less than two seconds on the flag sequence.' 'I know,' Zeus said. 'I've been watching.' Pause. 'You're going to be the best coordinator we've had.' Achilles looked at his food. 'I know,' he said. Then, after a moment: 'Zeus.' 'Yes.' 'I'm glad I said something. About the heel. Last season.' Zeus's crown listed left. Then straightened. 'So am I,' he said.
scenes.visual_prompt: Lunch scene, Zeus and Achilles, the rest of the group giving them space without making it obvious — Zeus not asking what the scrolls were about, just saying 'the coordination drills are getting better' — the genuine pride in Zeus's 'you're going to be the best coordinator we've had' — and then Achilles's 'I'm glad I said something' — Zeus's crown listing left, then straightening: the emotional weight of the moment making it do both — the two-movement crown, the rare straightening — Zeus's 'so am I' — the warmth of the exchange in its understatement — #FFD700 and lunch warmth, Zeus's crown detail visible

scenes.title: Archie's Third Scroll
scenes.narration: That evening, Athena found a fourth scroll on her desk. She hadn't put it there. It was tied with a small thread. She unrolled it. It was in Archie's... not handwriting. Archie didn't have hands. But there were marks — small, careful, made with a quill held in a beak. The marks traced the same trend data she'd been collecting. But they went back further. Before the first scroll. Before Achilles had said anything. Archie had been watching since before she started watching.
scenes.visual_prompt: Athena at her desk in the evening, finding a fourth scroll tied with a thread — unrolling it to find Archie's beak-marks: small, careful, unmistakably not human writing but clearly intentional — the data going back further than her first scroll, covering the period before Achilles said anything — Athena looking at Archie, who is on the desk, looking at her with his large golden eyes — the slow blink — Athena looking back at the scroll — Archie has been keeping records longer than she has — the lamplight warm, the scroll remarkable in its careful imprecision that is somehow exact — #3D2B00 and warm lamp amber, Archie's scroll detail the key visual

viral_hook: Athena has been keeping data on Achilles's ankle for two seasons. Artemis has been keeping her own records since the second week. Archie left a fourth scroll on Athena's desk — going back further than both of them. He'd been watching since before Achilles said anything.
highlight_note: Archie's fourth scroll — beak-marks, going further back than Athena's data. He was watching before anyone else. Before Achilles said a word about it.
short_title: Archie Was Watching Before Anyone Said Anything
rewatch_bait: Scene 4 — Artemis says 'since the second week. You weren't ready to know then.' Look at the second week episodes in Season 2. Artemis noticing the ankle in her training observation. She was building toward this conversation from EP022.
youtube_title: Athena had three scrolls of data. Artemis had a notebook. Archie had a fourth scroll going back even further. | Little Olympus S2 EP8
lesson: Being watched over isn't the same as being monitored. Athena, Artemis, and Archie were all tracking Achilles's ankle — but what they were doing was caring, in the specific way each of them knows how.
arc_notes: Achilles heel arc major development. The ankle is confirmed worsening — not reversible, only manageable. Achilles's response: stay on the coordination build, which is the right answer. Zeus-Achilles 'I'm glad I said something' is the emotional close of the S1 reveal paying off in S2. Archie's fourth scroll is the Archie mythology deepening: he sees more, earlier, than anyone. Zeus crown: lists left then straightens — the rare straightening marks genuine emotion.
